raw_case:
Sprengel's deformity
The features of Sprengel's deformity, also known as congenital high scapula, include abnormal positioning and dysplasia of the affected scapula, with a possible omovertebral connection or atrophy of the surrounding muscles. This case primarily aims to present the CT scan findings of a 19-year-old male with Sprengel deformity. Imaging enables the definitive diagnosis of this congenital malformation, and allows for the detection of associated anomalies, particularly the presence of the omovertebral bone, thereby facilitating appropriate management.

Introduction

Sprengel's deformity, also known as congenital high scapula or undescended scapula, first described by Eulenberg in 1863, is a rare congenital condition of unknown etiology, characterized by a failure of the scapula to migrate caudally between the 9th and 12th weeks of embryonic development. The diagnosis is typically made at an early age based on dysmorphic features or functional signs and is confirmed through imaging to ensure optimal therapeutic management. This condition is frequently associated with underdevelopment or atrophy of the surrounding muscles, resulting in a misshapen shoulder and limited range of motion [1,2].

Case report

We present the case of a 19-year-old male presented with a deformity of the right shoulder. The right scapula was found to be raised and fixed medially on physical examination. There was partially restricted movement of the shoulder joint. The patient had not previously undergone shoulder X-rays; 3 dimensional computed tomography (3D CT) examination was requested right away, which revealed abnormality, anterior curving of the supraspinous portion of the raised right scapula (Fig. 1). The horizontal and vertical lengths were almost the same in both scapulas. There was no omovertebral bone.The patient was referred to the orthopedic department for further surgical management, which consists of resecting the protruding portion of the scapula and translating it downward. This procedure aims to correct the elevation and improve both the aesthetic appearance and functional range of shoulder movement in our patient.

Discussion

Sprengel's deformity first described by Eulenberg in 1863, Sprengel described 4 cases in 1891 and lent his name to the condition [2]. Its pathophysiology remains unknown, but it is attributed to an interruption in the caudal migration of the scapula during embryonic development. Typically, the scapula is positioned between the second and seventh-eighth thoracic vertebrae along the posterior thoracic wall. Around 5 weeks of gestation, the scapula begins to differentiate near the lower cervical vertebrae. If it fails to migrate to its normal thoracic location by 9 to 12 weeks of fetal development, it results in an anomaly [[3], [4], [5]].Sprengel's deformity is characterized by abnormal positioning and dysplasia of the affected scapula, often accompanied by an omovertebral connection, wich is observed in 16% to 55% of cases [5]. It can form a bony, fibrous, or cartilaginous connexion between the superior angle of the scapula to the cervical or dorsal spine [6,7], but usually from the 4th to 7th cervical vertebrae [2].It is more common in females than in males, sex ratio is 1:3 [2,8] and may present from a young age with thoracic deformity, asymmetry, or limited shoulder abduction [3], and occasionally localized pain [10].Congenital scapular elevation is most often associated with various malformations, which can include rib anomalies such as aplasia, hypoplasia, bifidity, or supernumerary ribs, as observed in our first case. It may also involve clavicular malformations and, more notably, vertebral anomalies such as Klippel-Feil syndrome, hemivertebrae, scoliosis, and spina bifida, as seen in our second case. Additionally, it can be linked to extraosseous malformations, including diastematomyelia, cervical muscle hypoplasia, torticollis, cleft palate, and renal anomalies [[5], [9], [10]].Diagnosis is initially made through standard radiography, which reveals a high-positioned scapula and allows for radiological classification using the Rigault and Pouliquen system into 3 degrees: mild elevation (Grade I), moderate elevation (Grade II), or severe elevation (Grade III). The omovertebral bone can be challenging to identify on X-rays, so a CT scan with multiplanar reconstructions is used to confirm the diagnosis. This imaging technique helps to locate and measure the omovertebral bone, assess the scapula's position, evaluate the extent of the deformity, and identify any associated bony anomalies . MRI can be useful for detecting fibrous or cartilaginous bridges and for identifying associated medullary anomalies [1,[10], [11], [12].The complications of this condition are primarily aesthetic rather than functional, often involving thoracic deformity, the development of a posterior thoracic mass, shoulder asymmetry, and occasionally limited active shoulder abduction [10].Differential diagnosis includes neonatal brachial plexus palsy, winged scapula due to serratus anterior paralysis, high scapula from facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy, scapulopelvic dysostosis, or Klippel-Feil syndrome [10].

Conclusions

Congenital scapular elevation, or Sprengel's deformity, is a rare congenital malformation characterized by the abnormal elevation of the scapula. It is frequently associated with multiple other anomalies, with the most common being the omovertebral bone. The diagnosis is primarily clinical, based on the observation of scapular elevation and, in some cases, restricted shoulder abduction. Confirmation of the diagnosis and identification of associated malformations are achieved through imaging studies, which are crucial for ensuring appropriate management and treatment.

Author contributions

All authors contributed to this work. All authors have read and approved the final version of the manuscript.

Patient consent

Written informed consent for publication was obtained from patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 19-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Right shoulder deformity.
- **History of Present Illness:** The patient presented with a deformity of the right shoulder and partially restricted shoulder joint movement.
- **Past Medical History:** None reported.
- **Personal History:** None reported.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Noted a raised right scapula that was medially fixed with partially restricted movement of the shoulder joint.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 3D computed tomography (3D CT) showed an abnormal anterior curvature of the raised right scapula with no omovertebral bone noted.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnosis:**
   - Sprengel's deformity (congenital high scapula).
   - Neonatal brachial plexus palsy.
   - Winged scapula from serratus anterior paralysis.
   - High scapula due to facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
   - Scapulopelvic dysostosis.
   - Klippel-Feil syndrome.

2. **Rationale for Including/Excluding Diagnoses:**
   - **Sprengel's deformity:** The clinical findings of a raised and medially fixed scapula with restricted shoulder motion align with this diagnosis. Imaging confirmed abnormal scapular elevation and dysplasia.
   - **Neonatal brachial plexus palsy:** Typically presents with brachial weakness or paralysis, which is not described in this patient.
   - **Winged scapula (serratus anterior paralysis):** There was no mention of scapular protrusion during abduction or neurological findings indicative of serratus anterior paralysis.
   - **Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy:** This is a progressive weakness primarily involving the face and shoulders, without mention of progressive muscle wasting in this case.
   - **Scapulopelvic dysostosis:** This involves both scapular and pelvic abnormalities, which were not observed in the patient.
   - **Klippel-Feil syndrome:** No vertebral anomalies (e.g., fusion) were noted on imaging or discussed in the case report.

3. **Choice of Diagnostic Test to Confirm Sprengel’s Deformity:** 
   - CT imaging provided definitive evidence of scapular dysplasia, its abnormal curvature, and absence of omovertebral bone—solidifying the diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with **Sprengel’s deformity (congenital high scapula)** based on the clinical findings of raised scapula, restricted shoulder motion, and confirmed by CT imaging which showed scapular elevation and anterior curving without omovertebral bone.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. So, the patient is a 19-year-old male with a right shoulder deformity and partially restricted movement. The physical exam shows a raised right scapula that's medially fixed. The 3D CT shows an abnormal anterior curvature of the scapula but no omovertebral bone. Hmm.

First, I need to think about congenital causes. The raised scapula makes me think of Sprengel's deformity, which is a congenital condition where the scapula is elevated and often rotated. But wait, Sprengel's usually presents with an omovertebral bone in some cases, right? The CT here says there's no omovertebral bone noted. Does that rule out Sprengel's? Not entirely, because not all cases have that bone. The abnormal curvature of the scapula could still fit. Also, the restricted shoulder movement is a common symptom of Sprengel's.

Then, other differentials. Could it be a clavicle fracture or injury? But there's no history of trauma mentioned. The patient is young, so maybe a congenital issue is more likely. Muscular torticollis? That affects the neck muscles, but the scapula here is the main issue. Maybe a spinal deformity like scoliosis? The scapula elevation can be secondary to that, but the CT didn't mention vertebral anomalies. The scapula's anterior curvature is specific here.

Looking at the imaging results. The 3D CT shows an abnormal anterior curvature. In Sprengel's, the scapula is hypoplastic and may have a characteristic shape. The absence of omovertebral bone might make it less likely, but as I remember, around 30-50% of Sprengel's cases have that bone. So it's possible to have Sprengel's without it. Also, the medial fixation of the scapula suggests it's not mobile, which aligns with Sprengel's.

Other possibilities: acquired conditions like muscular imbalance or nerve injury? But with no history of injury or neurological symptoms, that seems less likely. The patient's age and presentation point towards a congenital issue. Maybe a congenital scoliosis variant, but again, the CT didn't mention spinal issues.

So putting it all together: young male, congenital deformity, raised and medially fixed scapula with restricted movement, CT findings of anterior curvature but no omovertebral bone. The most likely diagnosis is Sprengel's deformity, even without the omovertebral bone. Alternative could be a different congenital scapular anomaly, but Sprengel's is the classic one here.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a young male with a congenital right shoulder deformity characterized by a raised, medially fixed scapula and restricted shoulder movement. The absence of trauma history or family history suggests a congenital etiology.  
<step 2> Sprengel deformity (congenital high scapula) is a primary consideration due to the elevated scapula and restricted motion. Classic features include scapular hypoplasia, abnormal rotation, and possible omovertebral bone, though the latter is absent here.  
<step 3> The 3D CT findings confirm an anteriorly curved scapula without an omovertebral bone. While omovertebral bone is present in 30-50% of Sprengel cases, its absence does not exclude the diagnosis.  
<step 4> Differential diagnoses like clavicular anomalies, muscular torticollis, or spinal deformities (e.g., scoliosis) are less likely given the isolated scapular abnormality and lack of vertebral or spinal involvement on imaging.  
<step 5> The combination of clinical findings (fixed raised scapula, restricted movement) and imaging (abnormal scapular shape without omovertebral bone) strongly supports Sprengel deformity as the definitive diagnosis.  

### Answer:
Sprengel deformity (congenital elevation of the scapula)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a young male with a congenital right shoulder deformity characterized by a raised, medially fixed scapula and restricted shoulder movement. The absence of trauma history or family history suggests a congenital etiology.

Sprengel deformity (congenital high scapula) is a primary consideration due to the elevated scapula and restricted motion. Classic features include scapular hypoplasia, abnormal rotation, and possible omovertebral bone, though the latter is absent here.
The 3D CT findings confirm an anteriorly curved scapula without an omovertebral bone. While omovertebral bone is present in 30-50% of Sprengel cases, its absence does not exclude the diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses like clavicular anomalies, muscular torticollis, or spinal deformities (e.g., scoliosis) are less likely given the isolated scapular abnormality and lack of vertebral or spinal involvement on imaging.

The combination of clinical findings (fixed raised scapula, restricted movement) and imaging (abnormal scapular shape without omovertebral bone) strongly supports Sprengel deformity as the definitive diagnosis.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial differential diagnoses include Sprengel's deformity, neonatal brachial plexus palsy, winged scapula from serratus anterior paralysis, high scapula due to facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy, scapulopelvic dysostosis, and Klippel-Feil syndrome.

Step 2: Sprengel's deformity is included based on clinical findings of a raised and medially fixed scapula with restricted shoulder motion, confirmed by imaging showing abnormal scapular elevation and dysplasia.
Step 3: Neonatal brachial plexus palsy is excluded as the patient does not present with brachial weakness or paralysis.
Step 4: Winged scapula from serratus anterior paralysis is excluded as there is no mention of scapular protrusion during abduction or neurological findings indicative of serratus anterior paralysis.
Step 5: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is excluded as the patient does not show progressive muscle wasting involving the face and shoulders.
Step 6: Scapulopelvic dysostosis is excluded as the patient does not exhibit scapular and pelvic abnormalities.
Step 7: Klippel-Feil syndrome is excluded as no vertebral anomalies such as fusion were noted on imaging or in the case report.
Step 8: CT imaging confirms Sprengel's deformity by showing scapular dysplasia, abnormal curvature, and absence of omovertebral bone.
Final diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with Sprengel's deformity (congenital high scapula) based on clinical findings and confirmed by CT imaging.
